A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.

Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

sections.content:
LEFT moved the player from rows 24-27, cols 49-51 to rows 24-27, cols 45-47. So LEFT moved the player left by 4 columns. The player is now at rows 24-27, cols 45-47.
Target B is at rows 28-35, cols 16-19. Target C is at rows 28-31, cols 28-39.
The player is still 4 rows above target B/C and 26-31 columns to the right of target B, 18-23 columns to the right of target C.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT moved player from cols 49-51 to 45-47, same rows 24-27. Target B at rows 28-35, cols 16-19. Player still 4 rows above and 26+ cols right of target B.
Plan: Continue LEFT to move toward target B.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Continue LEFT toward target B
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT toward target B')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print("Player: rows [%d-%d], cols [%d-%d]" % (min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))

# Check targets
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print("Target B: rows [%d-%d], cols [%d-%d]" % (min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 28:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print("Target C: rows [%d-%d], cols [%d-%d]" % (min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player: rows [24-27], cols [41-43]
Target B: rows [28-35], cols [16-19]
Target C: rows [28-31], cols [28-39]
